title: Hanging woodchip or textured wallpaper
subtitle: Paste and hang a roll of woodchip or other textured paper for a paint-ready finish
excerpt: How to hang woodchip or textured wallpaper as a base for a painted wall. Covers paste choice, pasting the back of the paper, the soak time, hanging the first drop plumb, and the smoothing technique that does not crush the texture.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A starch-based adhesive sold as a powder for mixing with cold water, or as a ready-mixed tub. Heavy-grade paste is needed for woodchip and other textured papers; light-grade paste does not hold the heavier weight.

The time the pasted paper rests before it goes on the wall. The paste soaks into the paper fibres and the paper relaxes. Soaking time for woodchip is 10 minutes; lining paper is 5 minutes; vinyl is 3 minutes.

content.text: Woodchip wallpaper hides imperfect plaster and gives a paint-ready surface. The paper is heavier than standard lining paper and needs a stronger paste, more soaking time, and a gentle smoothing pass that does not crush the texture.
Step 1, prepare the wall

Strip any existing wallpaper. Fill any cracks and holes with a one-coat filler and sand flush when dry. Wipe the wall down with sugar soap to remove dust. The wall should be dry, smooth, and free of grease before the paper goes up.
Step 2, mix heavy-grade paste
Mix a heavy-grade
wallpaper paste
in a clean bucket as the packet directs. Stir well so no lumps remain. The paste should be a thick custard, not a thin soup; thin paste will not hold a heavy paper.
Step 3, mark the first drop

Measure the height of the wall from the top edge to the skirting. Add 100 mm at top and bottom for trim. Cut the first drop to length. Mark a plumb line on the wall, 550 mm out from a corner; the first drop hangs to this line so that the first edge is true, even if the corner is not.
Step 4, paste and soak

Lay the drop face down on a pasting table. Apply paste with a 100 mm pasting brush, working from the centre out to the edges. Cover the whole back of the paper. Fold the drop concertina-style with the pasted faces together so the paste does not dry. Set the
soaking time
timer: 10 minutes for woodchip. Paste the next drop while the first soaks.
Step 5, hang the first drop

Unfold the top of the first drop. Press the top edge to the wall with 100 mm of trim at the ceiling. Align the side edge with the plumb line. Smooth down with a paper-hanging brush in long strokes from the centre out to each side. Do not press hard; firm strokes are enough to bond the paper without crushing the woodchip texture. Work down the wall, unfolding the concertina as you go.
Step 6, trim top and bottom

Score along the top edge of the wall with a stiff plastic ruler held against the ceiling. Cut along the score line with the utility knife. Repeat at the skirting. The trimmed edge should sit clean against the ceiling line and the skirting top edge.
Step 7, butt the next drops

Hang the second drop with its side edge butted tight against the first. Do not overlap; woodchip butts edge-to-edge. Smooth down the same way. Continue around the room, working away from the natural light source so any small lip in a joint reads less. At external corners, take 25 mm of the drop around the corner and start a new drop with a fresh plumb line on the next wall.
Step 8, allow to dry and paint

Leave the paper to dry fully, usually 24 to 48 hours, with the heating off. Painting woodchip too soon traps moisture between the paper and the wall and causes peeling. Paint with two coats of matt emulsion, allowing each coat to dry. A medium-pile roller pushes paint into the woodchip texture; a short-pile roller bridges the chips and leaves them uncoated.
Variations

Other textured wallpapers (Anaglypta blown-vinyl, supaglypta) hang the same way but with the paste applied to the wall rather than the back of the paper. Read the manufacturer's instructions before pasting; the wrong method on the wrong paper will fail.
Care

Painted woodchip lasts ten to fifteen years before any maintenance. The most common issue is a loose corner at a door frame or window reveal; a dab of paste behind the corner with a small artist brush re-bonds it. Repaint as the colour ages.
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: Paste was too thin or the soak time was too short.
attrs.fix: Mix a thicker batch of paste. Lift the joint with a fingertip, apply fresh paste, press flat, and smooth in.

attrs.symptom: Woodchip texture is flattened along the smoothing path
attrs.cause: Too much pressure with the smoothing brush.
attrs.fix: Smooth lighter on the next drops. The flattened drops cannot be raised; they read as a gloss patch through the matt paint.

attrs.symptom: Paper bubbles in the centre of a drop after a few hours
attrs.cause: Paste was missing from one section of the back, or air was trapped under the paper.
attrs.fix: Slit the bubble with a craft knife in a small cross cut. Slide a thin smear of paste under the flaps with a teaspoon. Press the flaps back down and smooth flat.
